data.title: Oil And Equity Markets: Someone Is Lying
data.description:
The Market Is Thinking Fast. Oil Is Thinking Slow.

I am currently reading “Thinking, Fast and Slow” by Daniel Kahneman, the 2002 Nobel laureate in Economic Sciences. Kahneman explains, in his book, a universal truth that investors know all too well: we act first, then think. We make decisions based on instinct before we analyze. We find a simple explanation to make ourselves comfortable, and only later come back to the data. That’s essentially what I believe is happening today in the markets.

A crisis like the current Strait of Hormuz crisis is not another typical geopolitical event. It's not just another news item that will be over in two trading sessions. A disruption in the Strait of Hormuz is significant enough to affect refineries, inventory levels, transportation costs, inflation rates, central bank actions, and ultimately the P/E ratios of equities.

However, when looking at financial markets (specifically the NASDAQ, S&P 500), it appears as though nothing has happened. The NASDAQ (NDX) is acting as if the crisis was resolved, or never occurred. The S&P 500 (SPX) remains close to new highs. As always earnings are driving the conversation. The Brent Crude Futures Curve presents a logical story about a short-term shock followed by an adjustment in demand and then an increase in supply leading to normalized price levels. This is indeed a lovely story. However, is it even correct?

For now, it appears that the equity markets continue to rely on Kahneman's "fast brain": intuitive, emotional and eager for an easy explanation. The crisis will go away. Companies are beating expectations. Technology remains strong. If something breaks, the Federal Reserve steps in.

The physical crude oil market views the world through a very different lens than the equity market. The physical crude oil market is based solely on barrels. Thus it is based on ships, shipping lanes, inventory levels, available spare production capacity, logistics delivery times and real-world demand.

So, instead of focusing strictly on where Brent will be priced in one week, the much larger issue is: What happens if we mistake an improvement in investor attitudes toward crude oil for a true solution? What if demand destruction isn't a linear and orderly process, but a brutal adjustment that comes only after prices have already done their damage? What if the stock market is dancing on the Titanic due to investors having the incorrect paradigm regarding their risk exposure to potential losses?

This begins my analysis of this subject, which includes examining: the mathematics that governs the physical crude oil markets; the illusions of futures pricing; the disconnect between stock indexes such as the S&P 500; the Federal Reserve's "trilemma"; and three different operational scenarios to assist investors in determining what could happen during this time period and developing a portfolio that protects against losses once the rapid changes in the markets cease and the slow changing markets start to take over.

Oil Does Not Care About Narratives. It Needs Barrels.

To get a better understanding of how things are going with oil, you need to take some steps that may not make sense but will certainly help: stop watching the TV screens and looking at your graphs. And don’t bother trying to look into those complex models. Go back to the basics. Oil is not a "growth stock". Oil does not rely on earnings estimates, sales forecasts, valuation ratios, or stories. It has to exist as physical product. It has to leave its source, travel on a ship, arrive at a refinery, be stored and then burned every day.Every single day. Not in 12 months, not in the coming quarter. Every single day.

The formula is almost laughably simple and I went back to read it again when I saw it referenced in a recent report by JP Morgan:

Supply + withdrawals from inventories = consumption + increase in inventories

If the amount of crude entering the market decreases — either because of an interruption (as in a war) or a decision (as in Saudi Arabia cutting output, for example), the only way to prevent the system from breaking down is through one of three possible alternatives.

  • First: someone must produce more.
  • Second: the market must draw upon inventory levels.
  • Third: the price must rise to such high levels that users reduce their usage of oil.

In the case of the Hormuz crisis, however, the market is being forced to utilize each of the available options simultaneously.

The global oil supply shortage was at its highest level since the start of the year in both March and April. The deficit for the month of March stood at 9.1 million barrels a day while April's figure rose to 13.7 million barrels a day, massive shortages of oil supplies.

No matter how you spin it, this is an enormous gap between what the world wants and has access to. We're talking about one heck of a big hole in our daily supply/demand equation that needs to be filled on a regular basis.

Spare Capacity

If there is going to be some sort of cushioning effect to ease the pain of these shortages, then spare capacity would certainly be it. Ideally, OPEC and other alternative suppliers could step up their own production efforts and provide some relief to the marketplace. The irony in this story however is that many of the excess productive capabilities exist right inside or very close to the Persian Gulf region, considered by far the most logistically difficult area from which to draw upon. This situation can best be compared to having a fire extinguisher trapped inside a burning building: it isn't going to do much good.The U.S. can help alleviate the pressure on global markets but it cannot create enough capacity quickly enough. Additionally, I believe shale production should not be thought of as a switch. Increasing shale production requires weeks/months of planning, billions of dollars in capital investment, new drilling locations, completion operations, and logistical support. During this time frame, the market continues to require daily equilibrium.

Inventory Levels

As a result, we focus on another potential shock-absorber: inventory levels.

At this point, matters become even more difficult to track in terms of units and volumes. As apparent withdrawals from inventory levels were 4.0 million barrels/day in March, and 7.1 million barrels/day in April (see chart below), the global market is attempting to survive by drawing down inventory levels. While it is possible to continue drawing down inventory levels temporarily, it provides short-term stability rather than long-term solutions.

The Oil Market Has Split In Two

If you're an investor looking at Brent futures, you'll probably conclude that there is enough stability in the world to help resolve the present crisis through strategic negotiations and therefore we can safely say "the system will hold", "therefore we will eventually get past this". However, that is only one part of the equation. The second part of the equation is the physical market, which paints a very different picture.

Brent futures represent the financial market for oil. It is essentially the price of contract trades by both investors, traders and hedgers; and it is based upon what they believe oil may cost in the next several months.

Dated Brent is much closer to the actual, unvarnished cost of a barrel of oil. Dated Brent represents the price of near-term physical crude cargoes (that has to be physically delivered) in the relatively short-term. It does not provide a narrative; nor is it a prediction for the price of a barrel of oil in six months. Dated Brent is simply a representation of actual barrels of oil that have to be transported to refineries.

At the height of the most acute phase of the crisis, dated Brent prices were at record highs of over $140 per barrel, while futures prices had already plummeted, as you can see in the chart below. At various points during the crisis, physical oil was trading at premiums to futures prices ranging from $30 to $40. These are substantial discrepancies and indicate that financial buyers of oil futures are betting on some form of return to normalcy in the future, while those individuals purchasing actual barrels of oil currently have to pay for their lack of availability due to shortages.

It is quite clear why these two markets are diverging. Financial buyers of futures are highly responsive to headlines about cease-fires, negotiations etc., and therefore react rapidly to news. The financial market is also emotionally charged and very susceptible to narratives. On the other hand, the physical market doesn't respond to headlines. While physical barrels of oil sit in ports waiting to be loaded onto tankers, while tankers wait for favorable weather conditions and/or reduced insurance rates and while shipping routes continue to be blocked off and crude oil continues to flow abnormally from the Gulf, and while millions of barrels/day fail to materialize at refineries; the price of actual barrels of oil remains high.

So, if the physical market is right, then the problem isn't just oil above $100, rather, it could result in higher levels of inflation remaining sticky, reduce the amount of room available for cuts by the Federal Reserve, squeeze margins for corporations via rising energy costs and make the S&P 500 multiple far more fragile than it appears today.

The Curve Is Betting On A Miracle

Front-month-Brent is trading around $104–$105 per barrel. On the other hand, the 12-month contract is priced at roughly $79. So, basically, you're seeing a spread of roughly $25. What is essentially being said by the market is that crude oil is scarce now, but that in one year time the issue will virtually disappear.

This is backwardation. What is referred to as backwardation is the condition where the "now" barrel is valued significantly higher than the future barrel. By itself, this does not seem so unusual. A number of times in oil markets, during periods of supply tension, such conditions have developed. However, what is important here is the magnitude of the spread. The spread widths in question indicate that it is not just an indication of scarcity but also of a belief as to when the current shortfall will be resolved. In other words, based on the Hormuz Crisis, that the time frame of the crisis will be brief (short term), and that the diplomatic effort will bring about a rapid solution to the crisis and that the logistics system will quickly return to its normal pre-crisis state with minimal disruptions.

The story line appears to be logical and consistent. Unfortunately, the physical side of the oil market is not supporting it. While it is true that inventory levels may have declined, while it is true that a significant premium exists for each of the remaining barrels available in the physical market place and while it remains true that routes from the Persian Gulf region to Asia are compromised, these factors alone cannot confirm that the price for oil in 12 months ($79) represents some form of balance or equilibrium. Instead, this price represents a bet on rapid restoration to normalcy.

And it is here that the anomaly develops (which ties back to our prior discussion), because as previously discussed, the longer dated contracts along the curve suggest that all aspects of the current crisis will return to relatively normal conditions in a reasonable period of time. Conversely, the physical side of the oil market suggests that scarcity already exists. Not tomorrow. Not in an extreme case. Now.

Therefore, rather than ask whether backwardation is justified (it is, partially), we should be asking: How long can a curve that values oil at $79 in twelve months remain credible while the system continues to burn through supplies merely to exist?

If Hormuz were restored to its former self quickly enough, then we would find out that the curve was correct. But if this crisis persists for another couple of months or so, then the greatest risk is not that the value of the front-month contract declines. Rather, the great risk is that the entire curve increases. And when an oil curve moves sharply upward, it never stops at oil. It eventually impacts inflation, the Federal Reserve and corporate margins, before finally impacting equities, as I said before.

Equities Are Pricing Calm. Bonds Are Pricing Trouble.

To put the disconnect into sharper relief, we have so far maintained that the physical reality of oil is saying "there is a shortage," while the forward curve says "the normalization process will continue." And if you think that is extreme, consider that equity markets are actually pricing in a situation that is almost completely unrelated to the issue at hand.

The clearest indication of this can be seen through the VIX. At its peak during the most intense part of the shock, volatility had reached 31.05. However, instead of staying high, volatility then dropped to around 18-19. What this means is that the options market is no longer reflecting fear or panic, rather it reflects that the events occurring now (and likely to occur) are both contained and manageable, essentially normal.

It is here that the story becomes really interesting. For the VIX to drop below 20, for oil prices to remain above $100 per barrel, for the Strait of Hormuz to only partially reopen, and for there to be such tension in the physical market would indicate a level of confidence in the global economy. The market appears to be indicating that: "this crisis will not destroy the economy," "it will not harm our profit margins," "it will not harm the Federal Reserve," and "it will not harm the S&P 500 index."

The market may well be right. But it is making an extremely bold wager. Because, as much as everything connected to the digital economy exists within the cloud, it relies upon physical infrastructure. Semiconductors, data centers, chemical products, raw materials, transportation systems, fertilizer manufacturing processes, and logistics systems rely upon working trade routes, electric power, fuel supplies, and usable supplies of raw materials.

On the other hand, I'll be honest: I've always found real truth in the bond markets. They are the most farsighted and honest and reflect the truth of the market and the economy, in my experience. Equities are increasingly volatile and erratic, driven by flows that often pay little attention to these macroeconomic dynamics. Bonds, in my opinion, are not.

From approximately 3.95% to 4.31%, the 10-year Treasury rose in yield. From approximately 3.38% to 3.82%, the 2-year Treasury rose in yield. As a result, the yield curve has steepened ever-so-slightly with the long end experiencing greater upward pressure than did shorter maturities. This type of behavior represents a form of price action that is consistent with sustained inflationary pressures.

Three Possible Scenarios

To me, the key question is simple: what scenario is the market actually pricing?

Market Metric Scenario A: Fast Reopening (35%) Scenario B: Prolonged Dislocation (50%) Scenario C: Structural Shock (15%)
Time Horizon Strait reopened by mid-May. Closure lasts into Q3; normalization in Q4. Strait closed beyond Q3; physical damage to infrastructure.
Brent Price Peak above $120/bbl, then falls to $70–80/bbl by year-end. 2026 average around $95/bbl, with prices staying above $100/bbl for longer. Parabolic move, with Brent trading between $160 and $206/bbl.
S&P 500 Rally of 8–12%. Tech continues to dominate. Decline of 5–8%. Rotation into Energy and Defensives. Drawdown of 15–25%. Deep global recession risk.
Inflation & Rates CPI peaks around 3.5–4%. Fed cuts in Q4. CPI stays around 4–5%. Fed remains on hold through 2026. CPI above 5%. Monetary policy paralysis.

Equity prices, along with Brent futures prices, appear to be assuming that Scenario A is likely to materialize, i.e. that we can expect a relatively rapid reopening, and a corresponding rapid normalization of global petroleum product flow rates, resulting in lower oil prices by year end. In addition, these markets continue to assume that there will be opportunities for further monetary easing from the Federal Reserve later in 2026.

However, the physical petroleum market appears to tell a very different story.

As long as crude inventory drawdowns exceed a rate of approximately 7 million b/d, the current state of affairs cannot be viewed as a calm normalization of the petroleum supply chain. Such large drawdowns indicate that the petroleum industry is merely "buying time" in its attempt to stabilize the global oil supply and demand balance.

This situation does not necessarily imply that the current situation represents Scenario C. However, it certainly supports the belief that the market may be grossly underestimating the likelihood of Scenario B i.e., a longer term interruption to global petroleum supplies, higher levels of inflationary pressure, a Federal Reserve that is constrained from implementing additional interest rate cuts and an equity market that must reassess earnings margins, price-to-earnings ratios, and future growth prospects.

The Trade Is In The Physical Economy

In my opinion, the bottom line is that the markets are pricing-in what they believe to be a temporary crisis, while the physical commodity (oil) has already priced-in the reality of a shortage.

That said, if we assume that the futures curve is correct (i.e., Hormuz will quickly return to normal) Brent will drop back to $70-$80 and stocks will continue to "breathe", there’s an important fact that the markets seem to be ignoring: Even if a peaceful resolution were reached tomorrow, the time required to restart the global supply chain would likely be months.

The tanker traffic needs to start again. The insurance premiums will have to go back to normal. The shipping lanes can't remain closed for too long. Refineries need to receive crude regularly. The inventory levels are going to take time to build up. The spot/physical price of crude oil has to come back in line with the longer term contracts. So, an international agreement could help reduce some risks, but there is no way it can put millions of barrels per day back into the system overnight.

If the physical market is right, then the problem isn't just the price of Brent today. It's also that many of the financial ramifications from this occurrence (higher energy costs, higher logistical costs, stickier inflation etc.) could last well beyond any temporary reprieve from the immediate crisis.

  • The first natural positioning remains energy. However, not all types of energy are created equal. Specifically, you want companies that have production located in areas that are less likely to be affected by the current crisis with strong free-cash-flow generation and solid balance-sheet strength.
  • Secondly, the level of volatility (VIX < 20) in an environment that continues to see an unresolved energy crisis makes little sense. This doesn't imply that the stock market will experience a catastrophic collapse tomorrow. Instead, it simply indicates that hedge-buying opportunities remain relatively inexpensive versus the potential risk of damage that exists under the surface.
  • Lastly, as you consider reducing your exposure to increasingly crowded indices such as technology, keep in mind that technology is not entirely immune to events in the physical world. Technology relies upon semiconductor manufacturing; energy consumption; chemical products used in manufacturing; transportation systems; and the supply-chain. Therefore, when/and if the crisis persists, even companies with pristine track-records will incur increased costs and reduced visibility.

Ultimately, the uncomfortable truth is: at present, equity markets still appear to be buying the story of a quick return to normal. I’m not suggesting that it cannot occur. I am simply stating that the cost of that story-line appears too rich.

data.title: TransDigm: Commercial Exposure Drives Sell-Off, But Valuation Now Attractive
data.description:
Shares of TransDigm Group Incorporated (TDG) has lost over 10% since my last report. Stock prices had already fallen in February following the release of the first quarter results and the war in Iran has investors concerned on the impact of cost inflation. In this report, I revisit the TransDigm to assess whether the stock is too risky given cost inflation risks or whether the current share prices open up a buy opportunity for investors.

Commercial Exposure Is The Main Cause Of Concern

TransDigm has exposure to the defense market as well as the commercial airplanes market. Sales to manufacturers accounts for roughly 25% of the revenues while commercial aftermarket sales account for 32% of sales. That means that roughly 57% of sales are attributable to commercial aerospace and 43% to defense. In terms of EBITDA, the aftermarket business brings in roughly 75% of the profits. With the war in Iran, there are concerns that commercial aftermarket sales growth will stall and that is not an odd thought given that airlines are flying less. Less flying means less cycles on life limited parts or LLPs and pushes out demand for parts. At the same time inflation may rise and result in higher costs for not only the aftermarket business or only for the commercial business, but for the entire business where the company has no inflation escalators in place.

I note that for the upcoming quarterly results, analysts have increased EPS estimates by 3.2% compared to three months ago. The one-month revision trend, however, shows that in the three quarters after that EPS estimates have been revised down by up to a percent against revenue increases of up to 2%. While there is some cause for concern, I note that estimates have been holding rather well.

TransDigm Free Cash Flow Growth Prospect Is Impressive

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For TransDigm, I am expecting 10.5% annualized growth on sales through 2028 with 9.9% growth on EBITDA. So, that points at margins being down slightly from FY25 levels. Free cash flow, however, is set to grow nearly 20% annually. The company’s share prices are currently trading at 19.1x EV/EBITDA while the median EV/EBITDA for TransDigm is 21.9x and the peer group is even trading north of 24x EV/EBITDA. That is why I am maintaining my buy rating with a $1,537 price target indicating 32% upside noting that earnings for FY24 and FY27 are not even factored into the current share price.

The expectation is that EBITDA margins will be down in FY26 with incremental improvements in the years after. Free cash flow is expected to show strong growth with an increasing cash conversion ratio. We note that TransDigm does not pay a regular dividend, but occasionally pays a special dividend. Currently, I have not factors in any special dividends for the years ahead as I do believe it is bet for the cash pile to regrow and debt to be kept constant.

Conclusion: TransDigm Stock Price Is Oversold

I believe that TransDigm does face some risks regarding demand for commercial aftermarket sales, but at current prices we note that the stock is oversold not just against expected FY26 earnings but all the way back to 2024. As a result, I do believe that the stock remains a buy as the company is known for its M&A activity and if the market truly softens I do believe that TransDigm could acquire companies at better prices.

data.title: Tesla: The Street Wrongly Hates The Capex Plan, AI & Robotics On Track
data.description:
Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) reported a solid double-beat to kick off Mag 7 earnings season. But shares sold off as CEO Elon Musk pointed to high capex spending as the AI arms race presses on. The stock actually rose initially, but details on the Q1 conference call poured cold water on the top and bottom line figures.

Bigger picture, TSLA is the worst-performing Mag 7 equity year-to-date. Over the past year, the EV automaker’s shares have soared 49%, but optimism is all but gone after a tough stretch since last December. I had a "Buy" rating on TSLA back in February (after the Q4 report). The stock is down 11% since then, but I’m keeping my "Buy" rating. Today, I’ll provide a fresh look at the fundamentals, valuations, and technicals.

TSLA Now Lags The Mag 7 ETF YoY

In April, TSLA reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q1 non-GAAP EPS of $0.41 topped the Wall Street consensus forecast of $0.35, while revenue of $22.4 billion, up 16% from the same period a year earlier, was a small $190 million beat. Its gross margin came in at 21.1% (up a percentage point sequentially), while capex verified at $2.49 billion, also up from Q4 2025. Shares briefly traded higher post the report, perhaps due to a free cash flow beat, too.

But TSLA eventually dropped 3.5% by the close of the following trading day. It marked the second consecutive small post-earnings move and was well within the straddle’s implied change (5.5%). Looking ahead to the July Q2 report, the options market prices in a 6.8% earnings-related stock price swing based on the at-the-money straddle. Implied volatility is actually not all that high right now, currently 42%, according to Option Research & Technology Services. Shares of the $1.4 trillion market cap Consumer Discretionary company have a short interest of only 1.75%.

Looking back on the quarter that was, Tesla reported a strong start to the year, with revenue, earnings, gross margin, and free cash flow beats. On their own, that should have been good enough for the stock to at least meet the upper end of the implied straddle change. What's more, a significant highlight was the record growth in Full Self-Driving (FSD) subscriptions, which reached 1.28 million active users, signaling the early stages of autonomy monetization. Operationally, Tesla produced over 408,000 vehicles and delivered approximately 358,000 units.

While the company increased its aforementioned capex guidance to $25 billion to fund six new factories and AI initiatives, it is taking a cautious, safety-first approach to Robotaxi scaling, shifting its timeline to ensure rigorous validation ahead of the upcoming v15 software release. And, according to the Q1 report, production preparations are underway for the Cybercab, Tesla Semi, and Megapack 3, all of which are on schedule for volume production in 2026.

Color On Quarter

Looking forward, Tesla indeed expects unsupervised FSD to reach customers by year-end and plans to begin sales of its Optimus humanoid robots next year. More immediately, the firm targets Optimus production by late July or August and Robotaxi in a dozen states by Q4.

Finally, there is potential upside regarding the possible SpaceX IPO later this year if Musk seeks to roll up all of his ventures into a single entity (though that is just my speculation).

Tesla's Outlook

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to be very strong in FY 2026, with non-GAAP per-share earnings scaling $2. Further gains in the out year and through FY 2028 could result in $3.50 of non-GAAP EPS by FY 2028.

Those numbers have come down since my previous analysis, so my new price target range has been adjusted lower. Indeed, the sell-side has turned less enthusiastic with TSLA, as evidenced by a high 21 EPS downgrades in the past 90 days, compared to just 9 upgrades.

And while the FCF yield is low, TSLA sports a pristine A+ Factor Grade in the Profitability category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system.

Tesla: Revenue & Earnings Outlook, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, I noted last time Goldman’s 150x Q5-Q8 EPS estimate to arrive at its $400 target. BofA used a sum-of-the-parts approach, now seeing a fair value of $460. I err on the side of caution. If we assume a long-term PEG ratio of 5.25x, then using current EPS estimates, the stock should trade in the low $400s.

Of course, unlike blue-chip companies or even high-growth tech companies, a traditional P/E approach is difficult to figure with TSLA, and even a price-to-sales gauge offers little signal, given the high amount of value placed on AI and robotics that may only see sales and profits many years into the future.

PEG Ratio Now Below The LTA

Risks for TSLA include key-man risk with Elon Musk. If his attention is diverted or he is somehow disabled from leading the company’s innovation, that could lead to a steep valuation reduction.

Perhaps more in the here and now, increased competition from existing auto OEMs and EV makers may result in fewer Tesla vehicles produced and sold. Ongoing demand weakness is another headwind, though that is due in significant part to the EV tax credit’s expiration last year.

Investors must also watch capex trends, as the onus is on Tesla’s management team to execute on investment projects. Finally, FSD safety will be highly scrutinized in the quarters ahead.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show an unconfirmed Q2 2026 earnings date of Wednesday, July 22, for AMC. The company is also slated to give Q2 interim production data on Thursday, July 2.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares moderately undervalued in my view, TSLA’s technical situation is mixed. Notice in the chart below that while the S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 are at/near all-time highs, the stock price is well under the near-$500 double top. Moreover, take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average. It’s flattening, with the price below it, suggesting that the bears are gaining some control over the primary trend. Adding to the bullish-to-bearish vibes is the death cross that occurred earlier this month (when the 50dma moves below the 200dma).

Also see the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the chart. It has ranged in a bearish zone between 30 and 60. I’d like to see that range from 40 to 80, along with prices notching new highs. For now, support is down at $337 (the March low and the spot at which TSLA broke out during the mid-2025 consolidation). $490-$500 is clear resistance, while longer-term, more durable support is bolstered by heavy volume at prices below $300.

TSLA: $337 Support Confluence, $490-$500 Resistance, Bearish Death Cross

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on TSLA. The technical situation doesn’t excite me, and the higher capex is a gamble. Still, the growth-adjusted valuation appears attractive, while long-term optimism continues regarding FSD & autonomous, robotaxi, and Optimus.

data.title: Anterix: America's Spectrum Monopoly: The $7 Billion Asset At 89% Discount
data.description:
Why Anterix: The Investment Summary

Anterix (NASDAQ: ATEX) is unique. Anterix has a de facto monopoly on 900 MHz spectrum in the United States. The management, in their last call, valued the spectrum at $2.5 billion to $7 billion. Anterix's market capitalisation is only $750 million, and it has a net cash position.

No other company holds nationwide broadband-capable 900 MHz spectrum. The FCC is the barrier to entry - it would be almost impossible for a new entrant to gain this spectrum. On February 8, 2026, the FCC issued a game-changing ruling that extended the Anterix band, doubling its capacity.

The main current users of the spectrum are US utilities

Qualcomm announced a game-changing collaboration with Anterix on February 26, 2026. The aim is to develop a 5G chipset that could use the 900 MHz band in mobile devices. Every mobile phone can then connect and use the spectrum. When we asked when a similar partnership with Apple is coming, we were told: "Stay tuned".

To date, Anterix has over $400 million in contracts with 12 signed utility customers. Anterix claims it has a $3 billion contract pipeline. I believe that with the recent FCC ruling, the contract acquisition may accelerate. This week's announcement of a new contract may be an indication.

On April 14, 2026, Amazon announced $11.57 billion acquisition of Globalstar at 117% premium. The transaction shows that substantial premiums are paid for spectrum assets.

Morgan Stanley has been contracted by Anterix to run a formal strategic review. The aim may be to secure a deal similar to the one Globalstar secured for its shareholders.

History

Anterix was established in 2014, when the company acquired 6 MHz of 900 MHz spectrum from Sprint for $100 million. At that time, the company was called Pacific DataVision (pdvWireless). In 2019, the company changed its name to Anterix.

In 2020, Anterix got FCC approval for a 3x3 MHz broadband segment in the 900 MHz spectrum. In 2021, Anterix signed its first customer, Ameren. In 2024, Anterix appointed Scott Lang as its President and CEO. As of April 2026, Anterix has contracted 12 utility customers with contracted revenues of $400 million.

Why Is 900 MHz Spectrum Uniquely Valuable?

Not all spectrum is equal. 900 MHz has specific qualities:

  • Reach - low-band, like 900 MHz, penetrates structures better and travels further. A single 900 MHz station covers an area that would need six base stations for 3.5 GHz. This is important, especially for utilities operating in rural territories. The cost efficiency of 900 MHz is therefore much higher.
  • Ecosystem - 900 MHz has been standardised within 3GPP. Equipment from major producers such as Nokia, Motorola or Ericsson is readily available off the shelf. The Qualcomm partnership may bring the 900 MHz ecosystem to another level. Qualcomm chips are in almost every premium smartphone, including the ones produced by Apple. Adding consumers to its addressable market represents a big opportunity for Anterix.
  • Regulation - to qualify for a 900 MHz broadband license in any county, the applicant must hold at least 50% of the licensed spectrum in that county. Anterix now owns around 60% of all licensed 90 MHz spectrum. Anterix took decades to build its network through FCC auctions and private purchases. As of 2020, Anterix has achieved that threshold in 96% of US counties (3,100 of 3,233). The remaining 120 counties are mainly rural, less populated areas. "Complex systems" - operators with more than 45 integrated sites. No competitor can regain this position in 900 MHz.

Gamechanger: The February 18, 2026 FCC Decision

On February 18, 2026, the FCC ruled to expand Anterix's spectrum from 6 MHz to 10 MHz. This is a game-changing regulatory event for Anterix. This increases the data capacity the network can carry from 3G to 5G. Basically, from messaging to HD movie transmission is a very material event. The FCC ruling titled "Maximising the Potential of the 900 MHz Band" signals the spectrum quality upgrade from a utility-focused to a broader economic tool.

This was best illustrated on the April 9, 2026, investor call. Anterix CFO Elena Marquez stated that the ruling increased the value of Anterix's spectrum from the $1.5–$4 billion range cited for 6 MHz to $2.5 billion to over $7 billion.

Gamechanger 2 - The Qualcomm Partnership

On February 26, 2026, Anterix announced a new collaboration with Qualcomm to develop chips for the 900 MHz broadband.

Qualcomm chips are in almost all premium smartphones, including those from Apple. If 900 MHz chips are included in consumer devices, the addressable market would extend from hundreds of utilities to hundreds of millions of US citizens.

The next step is the cooperation with Apple. This will be a material catalyst for Anterix.

Strategic Buyers Pay Up for Spectrum

On April 14, 2026, Amazon announced the acquisition of Globalstar for $11.57 billion, a 117% premium. Amazon aims to combine Globalstar's spectrum portfolio with its Amazon Leo to establish a direct-to-device service.

The deal indicates that large technology companies pay very substantial premiums for quality spectrum assets. Anterix's near-monopoly on nationwide 900 MHz, 10 MHz expandable spectrum may represent a similarly valuable spectrum asset.

Globalstar's Q1 revenue was only $72 million. That is well below Anterix's $400M+ contracted revenues.

Most powerful companies are competing for valuable spectrum assets. SpaceX was outbid by Amazon in the run for Globalstar. Anterix's engagement with Morgan Stanley is happening in quite a favourable environment.

The Satellite-to-Consumer Opportunity

The main news on the April 9 investor call was the announcement of Anterix's partnership with Lynk Global to file for an FCC experimental license to test satellite-direct-to-device integration using Anterix's 900 MHz spectrum.

This is again is a very transformative step for Anterix. Lynk is, after SpaceX, the second licensed commercial service D2D provider in Guam and the Northern Mariana Islands. Lynk's technology allows smartphones to communicate directly with satellites. The opportunity is to utilise Anterix's 900 MHz licences for satellite connectivity for mobile phones in the US.

A good case study is Grain Management.

Grain purchased 800 MHz spectrum from T-Mobile for approximately $2.9 billion. Anterix's 900 MHz is one step higher frequency than Grain's 800 MHz, with superior propagation characteristics. Grain was initially targeting utilities but is now refocusing on D2D as well. If Anterix gains FCC approval for the satellite D2D, the target market would increase from utilities to 300 million American mobile users.

Spectrum Monetization

Anterix has two major revenue streams:

  • Spectrum leases - Anterix retains the license, utility pays for the right to use it
  • Spectrum sales - license transfers to the utility

In both cases, Anterix gets upfront cash payments

Utilities are regulated entities - they earn a regulated return on investments. Leasing payments are treated as operating expenses. Purchases of spectrum capital assets enable them to earn a regulated return on such investment. For these reasons, many utilities prefer to buy rather than lease.

Anterix has two additional revenue streams: - TowerX (with Crown Castle, 40,000+ tower sites) and CatalyX (SIM/eSIM management). These are the kind of service revenues that Anterix generates from its clients. The initiatives are in an early stage.

Financial Position

As of April 2026, Anterix had over $400M in contracted proceeds from 12 signed deals.

Management estimates the combined addressable market at approximately $1 billion annually.

The company had ~$38M cash balance and zero debt at the year-end 2025.

Value Creation Opportunities

There are ways Anterix can create value for its investors:

  1. Growth of its utility business - Anterix claims to have a $3 billion pipeline across 60+ potential customers in this segment. Signing up just 30–40% at past deal prices would generate $900 million–$1.2 billion. That is well above its current market cap.
  2. Strategic sale - Anterix has engaged Morgan Stanley for a strategic review. The Amazon acquisition of Globalstar indicates the upside potential. Candidate acquirer list includes infrastructure funds (Brookfield, DigitalBridge, Stonepeak), Crown Castle, Ericsson, Nokia, Qualcomm, and utility consortia.
  3. Consumer and Satellite expansion - the Qualcomm partnership and the Lynk satellite license open new opportunities for Anterix that would multiply its current $3 billion addressable market

Valuation

The current Anterix Market Capitalisation is around $828 million at $44 per share at the time of writing.

In November 2025, Anterix's CFO provided a valuation range of $1.5 - $4.0 billion for its 6 MHz spectrum. That would imply $180-$210 per share. That was before the February 18 FCC decision that increased the Anterix spectrum capacity to 10 MHz.

Anterix sold spectrums to utilities. We calculated Anterix's implied valuation using the five major sales. The implied spectrum value was $2.66 billion, which corresponds to $140 per share. Importantly, all the above deals were for the 6 MHz. Deals to be signed in the future for 10 MHz should have a substantially higher valuation.

On its April 9, 2026 investor call, the CFO provided a valuation range of $2.5-$7.0 billion for its spectrum. That would imply $130-$370 per share.

Another valuation data point is NextNav (NASDAQ: NN). NextNav holds approximately 15 MHz of lower 900 MHz band spectrum (902–928 MHz) and trades at a market cap of approximately $2.4 billion. NextNav has no utility contracts, generates no revenues from its spectrum and does not even have the FCC ruling it would need. Despite all that, NextNav's market cap triple Anterix's market cap.

We believe Anterix has the potential to multiply its current valuation. Our base case for the next 12 months is $130 per share.

Potential Risks

  • Slover execution - utilities are slow to make decisions
  • Strategic review may take longer
  • Grain Management potential competition
  • Slow process with the Lynk and Qualcomm partnerships
  • Anterix liquidity

Conclusion

Anterix is a unique opportunity - it has a near-monopoly in its segment, a strong base-case target market, and a very strong upside from the Qualcomm and Lynk partnerships, which are opening new markets that are multiple times larger than its current market.

Management estimates its spectrum is worth $2.5 billion to $7 billion. Anterix has a market capitalisation of $758 million. That indicates the upside potential.

The recent Amazon/GlobalStar deal indicates that spectrum assets are traded at high premiums. Anterix engaged Morgan Stanley to achieve a similarly strong deal.

All of the above are the reasons Anterix became one of the largest positions in our family office.

data.title: Are You Focusing On The Wrong Things In This Market?
data.description:
By Mike Larson

Transcript

Mike Larson: Hi there. I'm Mike Larson, Editor in Chief with MoneyShow, and I'm coming to you from the MoneyShow Masters Symposium in Hollywood. Today, I'm speaking with Matt Powers, President, Founder, and Managing Partner at Powers Advisory Group; and Kevin Simpson, Founder and CEO at Capital Wealth Planning. Gentlemen, how are you?

Matt Powers: Good.

Mike Larson: Okay. Sorry. I started with his introduction first when you're next to me. So, first of all, apologies.

Matt Powers: He's the important one here.

Kevin Simpson: I feel like we can roll with whatever you want to do?

Mike Larson: There you go. There you go. Well, guys, I'm glad you could take some time out here at the event to talk about what's going on in the markets. Kevin, I'm going to start with you. Crazy volatility. By the time this airs, we don't know what the headlines are going to be, but how do you navigate a market like this?

KS: Well, that's the most exciting part of the question because whenever this comes out, whether it's two days, two weeks, who knows what the news cycle will be, but we certainly had a reprieve this week. And that's why we're seeing markets rally as powerfully as we did yesterday. And even if we get back a little bit today or between now and the time that this airs, what we did was we took the worst-case scenario off the table. Anytime that happens, I feel like it's a really easy place to take a breath to think about things from the macro level, where we're not just focused on geopolitics or artificial intelligence, but we can focus on earnings. We can focus on the economy.

Next week, we have earnings season start. And I think that if we can put this Middle East conflict a little bit more in the rearview mirror every day, then the more we can focus on the macro and the things that matter.

Mike Larson: Got it. Matt, you've been in this business for a long time. It's all geopolitics all the time right now. But, again, how do you invest in that environment, and how do you look ahead to what happens after we maybe move this off the front page?

Matt Powers: I mean, I echo a lot of what Kevin said there. It's, I think you've got to take the geopolitics and almost, you almost have to push them to the side. We're starting earnings season. No banks next week. And get back to some fundamentals here. And, again, it's not – I think not because geopolitics right now are not important. Obviously, certainly are, but it's all speculation at this point, and we don't know. The headlines change every day.

In the absence of really good information, there comes speculation. That's where we're at right now. And I just think that there's forgotten parts of the market. I know Kevin talks about it a lot when he's on halftime. So, they've been growers, areas like that, yes, you have to own some of these Mag 7-type names. You have to own mega-cap tech, but at the same time, you have to couple that with some things that are, that have capital discipline. And that's – if I'm looking to invest, that's where I'm looking better at.

Mike Larson: Got it. Well, when we came into this market in 2026, prewar, you did see some rotation, right? You saw tech starting to wag. Some other sectors were picking up. Value was getting a little more attractive. Do you think that's going to resume? And is that your thesis for the year, or what do you find attractive here?

Kevin Simpson: First of all, I felt like Matt was calling my strategy boring. And I…

Matt Powers: It's mine too, so we're still in the same boat.

Kevin Simpson: And I think boring can be really good. Especially in light of what we've seen, maybe not since the beginning of the year, but really going back to the third quarter. Because if you think about this rotation, it wasn't a 2026 story. It's become one, but it was really the third quarter of 2025, and we started to see that there were other stocks in the marketplace beyond just seven tech names that would fuel the retail space, but with broadening out, I think we can really take advantage of that and become diversified portfolio managers.

And I love what Matt said about quality. So, if you look at cash flow, if you look at earnings, and you look at discipline, perpetual compounding through investments, it doesn't matter what's happening in the short term. It matters so much more what's happening in the long term. And I think that setup for 2026 is absolutely presenting some opportunity.

Mike Larson: Matt, I want to talk a little bit about, again, this value strategy, this dividend strategy. I mean, is it not – boring? Maybe. Yes. But, again, in this market, we have volatility…

Kevin Simpson: Those were my words, not him.

Mike Larson: Oh, okay. I'm not going to put words in his mouth. But, I mean, especially in an environment like this, that's important to an investor, correct?

Matt Powers: Yeah. Oh, yeah. Absolutely. I just did a session and talked a lot about this. I think it's very easy. And I think well, one of the issues I think for investors in general is, you own an ETF, you own a fund, you're already owning those names, period. If you own a large growth fund or just a large-cap fund, there's such heavy concentration at the top. I know this has been debated many a time, whether or not that matters, but it does. You already own that stock. So, if you own Mag 7, insert a couple other companies in there, outright – you also own them in your ETFs.

So, then you couple that with a strategy where it's like, hey, I want true capital discipline. I want companies that pay a dividend that grows. I want companies that consistently buys back shares of their stock, and they do it responsibly through free cash flow, and they're not taking on debt to do it. And that's a base of a portfolio.

If you're allocating your portfolio and you are against owning fixed income, you don't want to own fixed income, you've got to have some base in the side there. And we just saw, I think, that the sectors over the past three years that have performed the best are actually now at the bottom. And the ones leading this year are the ones that have lagged, sans energy, tech, energy are leading this year. So, and those have a lot of those names in there.

Mike Larson: Kevin, I want to ask you about where we stand in the AI tech cycle. Because I know at the beginning of the year there were a lot of questions. Too much CapEx, too much borrowing, too much spending, or not. You'd have people very strongly on one or the other side of the issue. What are your thoughts about where we stand?

Kevin Simpson: Well, I think time will tell. I mean, you're still rewarded for CapEx as long as you're generating it from free cash flow, playing off Matt's point there that if you have money to spend, it's fine to spend it. Maybe leveraging it is where we see some problems. You saw that briefly with Oracle (ORCL). If you look at how Oracle traded versus some of the other mega-tech spenders, it's still like free cash flow is king, and that's a lot different than what we saw in 2000.

So, we went from AI enthusiasm, where you could just mention AI on a conference call, and your stock would practically go up overnight, to a little bit of exhaustion. And I don't know that it's complete paranoia at this point, but it's really a show-me story. We want to see ROI. Can you prove that there's a return on investment? And if so, these companies will be rewarded because we know it's a game changer. And there's so much dislocation every single day from one sector to another that's being influenced by LLMs.

I think it's super fun to be in this business. I can't wait to see what happens. But if you focus on the fundamentals and you look out maybe three to six months instead of three to six years, if you can be nimble enough in your portfolio to make sure that you don't mess it up too badly.

Mike Larson: Got you. Matt, I want to ask you about the earnings outlook we talked about a little bit earlier. The focus is hopefully going to shift for that in Wall Street. What are you looking for in Q1, first half? How do you think the year is going to sort out?

Matt Powers: Well, I mean, you could almost tie that in with where we are, and this is really a side conversation, but tie that in with inflation, right? So, we got the read today. We have a read tomorrow and see where we stand with that and how oil is going to bleed into, really, into all parts of the economy, not just energy in general, but it hits transportation. It hits input costs. It hits all that. And I think this quarter's earnings are going to be important. I think next quarter, it won't. A lot of it depends on not bringing any politics in the conversation, but it depends on how long we're going to be at -- and then on to our next venture and what that's going to do to oil in general. What's that element?

Mike Larson: Fair enough. Kevin, another topic that one of our speakers brought up earlier, they did a poll, raise your hand how many times I think the Fed's going to cut one, zero. And somebody afterwards approached her and said, well, you didn't ask are we going to hike. Thoughts on the monetary policy? How important is that to your outlook for the year?

KS: Well, I'm going to once again play off Matt's comments because it really comes down to inflation. The inflation is going to be guided by energy prices in oil, and we're not going to see that as much in the first quarter. I certainly think second-quarter plus guidance will be more important, but it's not just the corporate expectations, margins, earnings. It's also the consumer. And we all feel it when we go to the pump. So, if you have an inflationary problem that moves forward into the end of the year, the rate hike story becomes a conversation. Stagflation becomes a conversation.

We're not there yet, and we don't need to be. If oil backs off and we're free of this in a few weeks, then it is a question of rate cuts. And we see from the Fed minutes this week that they're still looking for one rate cut. And maybe that's for posterity purposes and nothing else, but we, as stock market participants, love rate cuts. It's like, there's nothing better. So, if we can get one cut this year and maybe one cut next year because inflation cooperates, like, that's the perfect scenario. But no matter who you put in the chair, data dependency will always be, I think, the Fed's mantra.

Mike Larson: Got it. I guess the other thing I haven't talked about yet is midterm elections. I mean, how that's going to impact markets? Is it going to be a case where almost no matter what happens, Wall Street will just be happy that it's over?

Matt Powers: To answer that, I mean, short answer would be, yes. I think that, all the volatility starts. Again, it's just pure speculation, and it's the – stocks do not like the unknown. They don't like uncertainty. We know that. I mean, that's the basis of the stock market in general. And once we get through midterms, on at the end of the year. But I think leading up until that, I think a lot of – I think the focus is all going to tie back to oil, period. And everything's going to be oil, oil until we figure out where we're going to be with this entire situation.

Mike Larson: Okay. Wrapping this up, I guess, parting advice if somebody wasn't able to see your presentations, which they should have. But if they weren't, what would be your advice to investors watching this in this market?

Matt Powers: Don't forget other parts of the market. And I think we've already seen that for Q1. It's rotation and broadening, and you look under the surface, and you've already started to see Mag 7 versus the other 493 versus the S&P, and you've certainly seen other parts of the market pick up steam, and I would not forget that they're out there.

Mike Larson: Kevin, same to you.

Kevin Simpson: I think volatility that we're seeing in this market presents opportunities, and it's taking advantage of it. Separating the emotions. I'm afraid to do something to actually lean on the mathematics. When stocks are down is when you want to add to your positions. When things rally, it's a good time to trim. But doing nothing, I feel like, is probably the biggest mistake we can make as money managers.

Mike Larson: Got it. Perfect. Kevin, Matt, thank you so much for talking to me. Best of luck in the show.

Matt Powers: Yeah. Absolutely.

Kevin Simpson: Thank you.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Tesla Q1 Results: Sales Stagnate, Risky Ventures Burn Through Cash
data.description:
Elon Musk-led Tesla, Inc.'s (TSLA) release of its first quarter (Q1) earnings for its Fiscal Year (FY) 2026 after market close on the 22nd of April didn't immediately reverse the trend in the stock, which was down 14% relative to the start of the year as of the day before the release. The stock was essentially flat on the day of the release and went on to drop 3.6%.

However, there is potentially some qualified solace for long-time Tesla bulls: early trends in FY 2026 - strictly in balance sheet items - indicate that it will (at best) mirror trends in the previous two FYs, without any immediately apparent signs of worsening.

Trend Drilldown

Early trends indicate that overall revenue is poised to be weakly negative to flat relative to the past two FYs:

While the total cost of revenue is inching towards showing signs of improved efficiency - particularly in automotive sales, leasing, and energy - spends on R&D takes overall operating expenses towards at least a 20% growth over the previous FY - in line with general trends since 2023.

If early trends in the bottom line (i.e., earnings or net income) continue, net income shrinking will largely mirror the past two FYs, with a 48% reduction relative to the previous FY.

Interestingly, in terms of line item percentage share of total revenue, the company is arguably a picture of stability relative to FY 2025.

Like the previous FY, automotive sales and leasing account for a little over 70% of all revenue, while the likes of the Supercharging network, service operations on the massive quantities of vehicles sold over past years, and so forth continue to progressively inch up in meaningfulness towards revenue contribution.

In the overall production and delivery mix, little has changed since FY 2025. Model 3/Y continues to account for 97% of all vehicles produced, and overall production is almost exactly a fourth of volumes produced across FY 2025. Other models, however, spiked to 5% share of deliveries in the quarter and might possibly smooth out into the 3-5% share seen since 2021.

This "lack of worsening" did appear as a surprise relative to analysts' consensus estimates. While it ordinarily would have registered as a positive signal for the stock, the market likely digested the news in context; alongside an overall chill in new vehicle sales evident across the U.S. (Tesla's main market) and the massive premium the stock enjoys relative to other carmakers, there was little impetus to or appetite for the stock.

That is, if the company were to be considered a "carmaker" - a notion that Elon Musk has been hard at work in an attempt to dispel over the past several quarters.

New Directions While Digging In

A positive development in the quarter was a strong $1.44 billion in free cash flow. However, CFO Vaibhav Taneja cautioned that free cash flow will be negative for the rest of the year as the company spends on its ongoing endeavors that support Musk's bid to reframe the Tesla story: its ongoing work in AI, robotics, autonomous driving and - now - chip fabrication.

The earnings release highlighted that the Cortex 2 AI Training Compute cluster - needed for both autonomous driving and its humanoid robot project - is now online and has started training workloads. The first-generation production line for its Optimus humanoid robots - with a designed capacity of 1 million robots a year—will be replacing the Model S and Model X lines in Fremont and is scheduled to begin in Q2 2026. The second-generation line under development in Gigafactory Texas is designed for a long-term annual production capacity of 10 million robots.

In April, the company completed the final chip design of its next-generation AI5 inference processor and, in partnership with SpaceX (also led by Musk), is preparing to build the world's largest chip fabrication facility - one that will take the vigorous vertical integration paradigm Tesla has taken in vehicle manufacturing into the chip space by integrating logic, memory, and packaging.

In the vehicle space, the company also announced that work continues in its new battery and material factories, which span from LFP cells in Nevada to cathode material and lithium refining in Texas. However, the company also states candidly that battery pack capacity continues to be a limitation on vehicle production ramp-ups.

In line with the upcoming launch of the Tesla Semi - the all-electric Class 8 semi-trailer truck designed for freight transport - the company also announced that it will be deploying public Megachargers, ultra-fast, high-power charging stations specifically designed for the Tesla Semi.

In the Q4 2025 report, the company has indicated a prospective map for Megacharger locations in 2026.

California and Texas are projected to have the highest density of these facilities as of now.

In Conclusion

The overall picture is decidedly a mixed bag. The large-scale commitment to manufacturing humanoid robots designed to supplant or augment human workforces is a bold and risky bet, especially when the question remains if the combined cost of AI compute and the robot fleet truly provides a cost differentiator against human labor.

Also in question - and in roughly similar terms - is the repeated commitment to building a fully autonomous Cybercab arm. While operating a "robotic Uber" is attractive on paper, regulatory approvals for large-scale deployment are still far away and a sizeable roadblock to surmount.

On the other hand, owning and operating a chip fabrication facility alongside a design lab is a definite long-term positive that opens up multiple addressable markets regardless of whether AI for robots or autonomous driving works out or not.

Another positive in a similar vein is the buildout of battery manufacturing for both vehicles and energy grid solutions. Electric Vehicles (EVs) are here to stay, and owning strong capabilities opens up interesting avenues in monetization in the future.

While the production of the Semi has been long-awaited, the relative paucity of charging infrastructure across the length and breadth of the land is poised to limit adoption in much the same way it did for passenger vehicles. While corporate fleet solutions are a tidy supplier demand, there is still a cap on competing with or supplanting the diesel-powered armadas of trucks rolling through the world's highways. A long ramp can be expected before the Semi becomes an attractive proposition worthy of consideration for investors.

However, in present-day terms, the fact remains that early trends in production volume stability indicate that the company is at an impasse in passenger EVs while other brands are registering some growth despite an outlook indicating a potential chilling effect looming on vehicle sales in the U.S. All in all, it's a company promising potential like it did during the 2010s while the market expects a steady translation of market share into value, and the company sinks cash into new, bold, and potentially risky endeavours.

All in all, a hold is in order.

data.title: Norfolk Southern: Watch It, But Union Pacific Is The Better Trade In The Deal Now (Earnings Review)
data.description:
Introduction

Norfolk Southern (NSC) just released its Q1 2026 earnings. Yet, the most important report for the stock was not its own but Union Pacific's (UNP). As the latter jumped up 7%, so did NSC. Since July 29, 2025, the two stocks have been more and more correlated. This makes sense because under the UNP-NSC deal, the math implies that NSC shareholders will receive, upon closing, 1 UNP share and $88.82 cash, which means that the implied value for NSC was $326 per share. The market is repricing the deal closure probability, and the more this happens, the more NSC trades up and down with UNP because their dollar moves converge.

However, in percentage terms, NSC should move a bit less than UNP because part of the deal is cash, and cash doesn't move with UNP's stock price. Once the deal was announced, there was room for an arbitrage play, where NSC shares were basically a call option on the deal closure. Today, the optionality on the deal is not cheap, and buying NSC today is more similar to a short put with a $4-$5 premium to absorb a risk of $88 per share if the deal breaks. This is not the payoff structure that people signed up for when they became NSC shareholders. If the deal closes, however, there is no real risk. Personally, I think the refiling the STB requested is actually a favorable event for the birth of the first American transcontinental Class 1 railroad. So, I don't think NSC shareholders should worry that much.

So, while I don't think NSC is a buy right now because of the pending deal, I still think that it is important to keep monitoring how the railroad is doing, both because it could soon be a key component of a new railroad and because it can tell us updated numbers on important economic activity in the country.

Norfolk Southern's Q1 2026 Earnings Review

When we look at NSC's results, we see that revenues were flat YoY to $3B. Merchandise reported volume growth of 1%, Intermodal was down by 4%, while coal, unsurprisingly, jumped up by 9% YoY. The total is that volume reported a 1% reduction, even though the revenue per unit increased by 1%. This means that pricing held.

When we look at the financial figures, we see something that is not exactly positive. Operating expense increased by 1%, the operating ratio (adjusted for the Eastern Ohio incident) deteriorated by 80 bps, and moved up to 68.7%. Norfolk Southern's operating income decreased by 2% to $939M, the same percentage decline as the company's net income, which was $597M. The diluted EPS were down by $0.04 YoY to $2.65, a 1.5% compression.

The CEO's remarks pointed out that severe weather was an issue. But I tend not to overweight this factor: weather will always be an issue with its unpredictability and volatility. However, it is true that Q1 is usually the weakest quarter. This means that operating expenses inflate if severe weather impacts rail traffic.

Let's take a moment to look at the commodity breakdown. We see that, beyond coal, the second best-performing commodity was chemicals. This is interesting because chemicals is usually used as a leading indicator of manufacturing activity. Since we have seen the manufacturing index move up in recent months, NSC offers us further confirmation of a trend that could be important for 2026.

The outlook that NSC's earnings imply is that the company could reach FY26 EPS around $11.50, which is $0.50 lower than the consensus. This is because I think that there will be three drags that need to be considered: merger expenses that are loading, customers choosing to dual-source while waiting for regulatory approval, and intermodal softness. Using an 18.5x earnings multiple, we have an implied share price of $213.

Last year, NSC ended up with $2.2B in FCF. This year, NSC announced that it will reduce its CapEx to $1.9B, which makes sense: if the deal goes through, CapEx will then have to be deployed for the integration. This is something that makes sense. After all, a merger is extremely difficult among railroads, which are companies that own and operate infrastructure. To rationalize the network, harmonize the operating systems, and upgrade the terminals across the railroad, CapEx will be needed, probably above normal levels. Therefore, budgeting only $1.9B in capex for this year is a way to create extra flexibility by preserving FCF in anticipation of regulatory approval.

So, NSC on a standalone basis is worth around $213. If the deal breaks up, there is a reverse termination fee that could be worth around $11 per share. This means that the floor in case the deal breaks is $224. This is 30% below the current price, implying a downside close to $100, while only leaving a 1.4% arbitrage spread. This means that buying NSC today is similar to collecting a $4 premium to absorb a loss of $100. This is a reward-to-risk ratio of 1:20, which makes no sense.

I was a shareholder of NSC, as I bought it some time after the share price plummeted due to the consequences of the Eastern Ohio incident. However, I didn't have the time to build a full position. Therefore, I sold it a few months ago as I was taking some gains from smaller positions for two reasons. I was consolidating and concentrating my portfolio, but I was also raising cash as I am about to buy a new home.

Right now, it doesn't make sense to buy NSC. Investors should just wait for the STB to clear the horizon and then make a new decision as to what to do.

If one believes the deal won't go through, UNP is a much better option. If one believes that the deal will be approved (I am on this side of the camp), UNP is still the better option because it has less risk than NSC right now. Basically, if we use game theory, UNP is the dominant strategy for new capital regardless of what scenario materializes.

data.title: Buy 14 Ideal Graham Value All-Stars Of 27 'Safer' Dividends In 71 April/May GVAS
data.description:
Foreword

  • This article is one of 15 Dividend Dog collections features in Seeking Alpha by this reporter who tags himself, the "Dividend Dogcatcher."
  • I am Fredrik Arnold and, as the Dividend Dogcatcher, I have devised a series of twenty keystone essays that change little but for the essential graphic and statistical daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly, or annual data that support a cadre of over 30,000 followers.
  • Those fifteen keystone Dogcatcher essays include the following indices and publications:
  • Dogs of the Dow
  • S&P500
  • S&P600
  • Berkshire Hathaway
  • Fortune 500
  • Kiplinger Reports
  • Aristocrats
  • Dividend Kings
  • Barron's Pro Picks
  • Reader Favorites & /Rogues (ReFa/Ro)
  • Sustainables (Barron's)
  • Bloomberg Intelligence
  • Power Dividends
  • Ben Graham Value All Stars (GVAS)
  • Monthly Payers (MoPays)

To learn the 14 Ideal Graham All-Star equities paying "safer" dividends, see the conclusion of this article, in the Afterword. (You might even start at the afterword and work your way back to the front.)

About Large Cap Value

"A Value ranking for large cap stocks from YCharts puts together complementary strategies found during stock research. The value ranking looks at the price of a stock relative to a number of measurements that determine intrinsic firm value. Only the largest 10% of companies based on market cap are shown in this portfolio.

What it is: The Value Score is a composite score. It tells you how much you are getting in terms of profits, cash flows, assets, sales, etc. for the stock price that you pay. It is a relative measurement, so it says nothing about the overall level of the market. Rather, it answers the question: "Given the current market level, which stocks give you the most current value for your dollar of investment?" Tens are the most value, and 1's are the least.

How to use it: Use it to find companies that are selling at a low price relative to their assets and profits.

Limitations of the Value Score: Watch out for companies with a lot of uncertainty or bad prospects for the future (e.g., Pharma companies with expiring patents, industries on the decline, etc.).

About the Ben Graham Formula

The Ben Graham Formula strategy contains ultra-stable stocks that will infrequently lose money if held over a long period of time. It was developed based on a screen in Graham's book, "The Intelligent Investor." For those who have the book, it is the "Defensive Investor" screen. It selects stocks that are large in terms of sales and total assets, have a strong track record of earnings and dividend payments, have a reasonable current ratio and level of long term debt, and have a low valuation given by PE Ratios and Price to Book Value ratios." -YCharts

While over half of this collection of GASV is too pricey, or reveals somewhat skinny or no forward looking dividends, fourteen of the twenty-seven "safer" lowest-priced dogs of the GASV are buyable.

April/May data found: BW LPG (BWLP), IRSA Inversiones (IRS); Weibo Corp (WB); Altria Group; Global Ship (GSL); Banco Bilbao Vizcaya Argentinaria (BBVA); Verizon (VZ); Main Street Capital (MAIN); Equinor ASA (EQNR); AT&T (T); Orix (IX); Deutsche Bank (DB); NuSkin Enterprises (NUS); Costamare (CMRE), on the buy-list.

[See a summary of top ten fair-priced April/May GASV Dogs (below actionable conclusion #21 mid-article) and all fourteen safe buys at the bottom of this article.]

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 24.48% To 109.05% Net Gains From Top-Ten GASV Dogs By April/May, 2027

Seven of ten top GASV (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this April/May, 2026 yield-based forecast, as graded by Wall St. wizard estimates, was 70% accurate.

Estimated dividend-returns from $1000 invested in the ten highest-yielding stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target-prices, as reported by YCharts, defined the 2026-27 projections below. (Note: one-year target-prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected for April/May, 2027 were:

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs".

Thereafter, four energy members entered the top ten, placing second, sixth, eighth, and tenth: BW LPG Ltd [2]; Alliance Resource Partners [6]; MPLX LP (MPLX) [8]; Energy Transfer [10].

Dividend Vs. Price Results

Remember, this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own it. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pull-backs in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Analysts Forecast A 21.30% Advantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 GASV As of April 20, 2026

Five higher-priced GASV top-yield dogs as of March 10 were: BW LPG, Energy Transfer, Ares Capital, Alliance Resource, and MPLX LP, with prices ranging from $18.54 to $55.69.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As the new year and March madness dies and the feckless Tech-fueled market inflation and dithering presidential deals gain momentum into 2026, with the exception of the few memes, look for more GASV to become fair-priced… slow and sure, or very suddenly.
